name: Java
description: Object-oriented programming on the JVM: classes, collections, exceptions, and modern language features.

content.value: Java source compiles to bytecode executed by the JVM. Entry point is public static void main(String[] args) inside a class whose name matches the public file name.
javac compiles .java to .class; java launches the main class. Modules (Java 9+) add stronger encapsulation at the package graph level.
Primitives (int, double, boolean, ...) are value types; wrapper classes box them for collections and generics. Prefer primitives in hot loops to reduce allocation.
var (local type inference) keeps code concise but still static—types are inferred at compile time.

content.code: var list = java.util.List.of("a", "b");

mcqs.question: What file name must a public top-level class use?

theory.question: Why does Java compile to bytecode instead of native code immediately?
theory.answer: Bytecode is portable across operating systems; the JVM can optimize at runtime (JIT), profile hot code, and apply safety checks before execution.

coding.title: Sum command-line integers
coding.description: Write a main that parses args as integers and prints their sum; handle NumberFormatException.

content.value: Classes bundle state (fields) and behavior (methods). Constructors initialize instances; use private fields with accessors to preserve invariants.
Immutability (final fields, no setters) simplifies reasoning in concurrent programs.

content.code: public record PointR(int x, int y) {} // Java 16+

content.value: Interfaces define contracts; default methods provide shared behavior. Abstract classes capture partial implementations and state.
Prefer composition over deep inheritance hierarchies—inherit for true is-a relationships.

content.code: // Strategy pattern via interface injection

mcqs.question: Which keyword prevents subclassing a class?

theory.question: Interface vs abstract class?
theory.answer: Interfaces define behavior contracts (often without state) and support multiple inheritance of type. Abstract classes can hold fields and partial method bodies while still forcing subclasses to complete the type.

coding.title: Shape area calculator
coding.description: Define interface Shape with area(); implement Rectangle and Triangle; print areas from a list.

units.title: Collections and generics
units.summary: Lists, maps, sets, and type-safe APIs.

content.value: ArrayList provides indexed access with amortized O(1) append; LinkedList wins for frequent middle inserts but loses cache locality. HashSet offers O(1) average membership.
equals and hashCode contracts must align for HashSet/HashMap correctness—use IDE generators or records carefully.

content.code:
Set<String> s = new HashSet<>();
s.add("x"); s.contains("x");

content.value: HashMap provides expected O(1) get/put; TreeMap keeps sorted keys. Generics erase to raw types at runtime with casts inserted by the compiler.

content.code:
Map<String, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
for (var w : words) freq.merge(w, 1, Integer::sum);

content.value: Wildcards (?, ? extends T, ? super T) increase API flexibility for producers/consumers.

content.code: static <T> void copy(List<? super T> dst, List<? extends T> src) { ... }

mcqs.question: Which collection preserves insertion order as of common JDK implementations for LinkedHashMap?
Generics in Java are primarily enforced at:

theory.question: Why implement both equals and hashCode for HashMap keys?
theory.answer: Equal objects must have equal hash codes so buckets agree; inconsistent implementations break map invariants and cause lost entries or duplicates.

coding.title: Word frequency
coding.description: Read tokens and print the top 3 most frequent words using a map and sorting.
coding.hint: Map<String,Long> or Integer; stream sorted by value.

content.value: Throwable splits into Error (usually unrecoverable) and Exception. RuntimeException is unchecked; other checked exceptions must be declared or handled.
Don’t catch Throwable broadly in libraries—convert to domain-specific exceptions at boundaries.
java.nio.file Paths/Files provide modern file operations; try-with-resources closes Closeable handles automatically.

content.code:
import java.nio.file.*;

String text = Files.readString(Path.of("in.txt"));

content.value: For large files, stream lines instead of loading entire contents into memory.

theory.question: When are checked exceptions controversial?
theory.answer: They force handling at every layer, which can lead to boilerplate throws chains. Many APIs prefer unchecked exceptions for fault-tolerant boundaries while documenting failure modes clearly.

coding.title: Safe file copy
coding.description: Copy a text file using Files and handle IOException with a clear message.

content.value: Java threads map to OS threads (on most JVMs). Prefer executor services over raw Thread for pooling and lifecycle management.
Shared mutable state needs synchronization—locks, atomics, or confinement—to avoid data races.

content.code:
private final Object lock = new Object();
synchronized (lock) { /* critical section */ }

content.value: ConcurrentHashMap offers segmented locking; CompletableFuture composes async pipelines; CountDownLatch coordinates phases.

content.code: var map = new java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer>();

content.value: Immutability and message passing reduce lock contention—design with failure in mind.

content.code: // Virtual threads (Project Loom) simplify blocking IO at scale

mcqs.question: Which is preferred for many short tasks?
A data race happens when:
options: Unsynchronized concurrent access to shared mutable state

theory.question: What does volatile guarantee?
theory.answer: Visibility of writes across threads and ordering constraints for accesses to that variable—not atomicity of compound operations like increment unless using atomics or locks.

coding.title: Parallel sum
coding.description: Split an int array into chunks and sum using an ExecutorService; compare with a single-thread sum.

units.title: Modern Java features
units.summary: Records, sealed types, pattern matching highlights.

content.value: Records are transparent carriers for immutable data—constructors, equals/hashCode/toString generated. Ideal for DTOs and algebraic-style modeling.
Validation in compact constructors keeps invariants without boilerplate.

content.code:
var u = new User("Ada", 100);
System.out.println(u.name());

content.value: Sealed types restrict which classes may extend them—useful for exhaustive switch modeling when combined with pattern matching.
Exhaustive switches help the compiler warn when cases are missing—improving maintenance.

theory.question: When might you avoid records?
theory.answer: When you need extensive mutability, complex identity semantics, or inheritance hierarchies that don’t fit the nominal record model—use regular classes.

coding.title: Model JSON-like tree
coding.description: Sketch sealed interface Json with records JsonNull, JsonBool, JsonNum, JsonStr, JsonArr, JsonObj.
